pages.full_text:
arrived with Olive. They played cricket / foot ball at the campsite. Then we all met up at a restaurant in Chester for lunch. Peter + I cycled back with Gigi to the campsite where Gigi + I had naps. Peter went back into Town with the others + they saw the Cathedral + a model railway (west coast main line) + arch building. And shopping.
In the evening Thomas cooked another lovely BBQ.

__Wednesday__
We waited while the Tents dried + Thomas + Peter packed up. In leaving Thomas went to look at camping vans. They all went home. Simon + Eve went west to near Harlech.
   We had trouble finding a lunch place along the Dee estuary. We saw an old ship with a red + back funnel. Travelling inland we passed Dolwyddelen Castle. 1283 it fell to Edward I.
pages.notes: Page number not visible in top-left corner — likely continues from previous page. 'back funnel' likely means 'black funnel'. Dolwyddelen Castle spelled 'Dolwyddelen' in the diary (standard spelling is Dolwyddelan). Year cannot be determined from this page alone; volume covers 2003–2019.

pages.full_text:
We stopped for tea near a pass before a big slate mine. Llan Bleanow Ffestiniog. Then onto the campsite south of Harlech, Llandanwg village. Glan y Gors campsite. A short walk away is a [?B] chapel, sunk in the dunes, St. Tanwg arrived there in the [?6].
We were quite tired after being with the family.

__Thursday__
   We walk behind the dunes in the salt marshes. They are protecting the marram grass which takes 5 years for a [?big] clump to get established. Lunch - cheese pasties + later tea time ice cream at Y Maes Cafe. Well run. V. busy. V. V. windy in the afternoon, keeping the skylight shut. Families on the sandy beach.

__Friday__
   We left before breakfast to get a space in the carpark, but there was a 2 m baffle, so we are
pages.notes: Handwritten page number 54 visible top-right. 'Llan Bleanow Ffestiniog' likely refers to Blaenau Ffestiniog — diary spelling preserved. '[?B] chapel' — the circled letter before 'chapel' looks like a 'B' or '6'; St. Tanwg is a known chapel at Llandanwg sunk in dunes. '[?6]' after 'in the' likely refers to a century (e.g. '6th century') but numeral is unclear. '2 m baffle' may mean a height barrier. Year cannot be determined from page content alone.
